Which of the following is the correct order of bond length among these compounds :
questions.topicName: Tetravalence of Carbon: Shapes of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation:
The order of C-C bond lengths is determined by the type of bonding and the presence of substituents.
1. Compound I has a C=C double bond adjacent to an ether group (–O–), which can stabilize the double bond, leading to a longer C-C bond compared to other compounds with stronger double bonds.
2. Compound II has a C=C double bond with a nitro group (–NO2) that can withdraw electron density, making the double bond stronger and thus shorter than in compound I.
3. Compound III has a C=C double bond with a chlorine substituent (–Cl), which is less electron-withdrawing than a nitro group, resulting in a slightly longer bond than in compound II.
4. Compound IV is an alkene with a C=C double bond without any substituents, making it the shortest bond due to the absence of any electron-withdrawing effects. Thus, the order of C-C bond lengths from longest to shortest is I > II > III > IV.

questions.question: Among the following molecules, the correct order of {tex}{C}-{C}{/tex} bond length is:
questions.topicName: Tetravalence of Carbon: Shapes of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation:
The order of C-C bond lengths is determined by the type of bonding present in the molecules.
1. C2H6 (ethane) has single C-C bonds, which are the longest due to the presence of only sigma bonds.
2. C6H6 (benzene) also has single C-C bonds, but due to resonance, the bond lengths are slightly shorter than in ethane, making them comparable.
3. C2H4 (ethylene) has a double C=C bond, which is shorter than a single bond due to the presence of both a sigma and a pi bond.
4. C2H2 (acetylene) has a triple C≡C bond, which is the shortest due to the presence of one sigma and two pi bonds. Thus, the correct order of C-C bond lengths from longest to shortest is C2H6 > C6H6 > C2H4 > C2H2. This reasoning aligns with the solution provided.

questions.question: Which of the following is maximum stable :
questions.topicName: Tetravalence of Carbon: Shapes of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution identifies conjugated alkadienes as the most stable due to their ability to delocalize electrons across the double bonds. In conjugated systems, the p-orbitals overlap, allowing for resonance stabilization, which lowers the overall energy of the molecule. In contrast, isolated alkadienes have double bonds separated by single bonds, limiting resonance and electron delocalization. Cumulated alkadienes have adjacent double bonds that do not allow for effective overlap of p-orbitals, resulting in less stability. Thus, the conjugated alkadiene is the most stable due to its enhanced resonance and electron delocalization compared to the other types of alkadienes.

questions.question: Among these compounds, the correct order of {tex}{C}-{N}{/tex} bond length is :
questions.topicName: Tetravalence of Carbon: Shapes of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation:
The order of C-N bond lengths is determined by the type of bonding and the presence of resonance or hybridization in the compounds.
1. Compound III (CH2=NH) has a double bond between carbon and nitrogen, which shortens the bond length due to increased overlap of orbitals.
2. Compound II likely has a single bond but may have some resonance or inductive effects that can slightly lengthen the bond compared to a pure single bond.
3. Compound I has a single bond, which is longer than a double bond but shorter than a bond that is influenced by weaker interactions.
4. Compound IV likely has the longest C-N bond due to the presence of more electronegative atoms or steric hindrance, which can increase the bond length. Thus, the order of bond lengths from shortest to longest is III > II > I > IV, making the solution correct.

questions.question: The dihedral angle between two methyl groups in partially eclipsed conformation of n-butane is
questions.topicName: Tetravalence of Carbon: Shapes of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The dihedral angle between two methyl groups in the partially eclipsed conformation of n-butane is indeed 120 degrees. In this conformation, the methyl groups are not fully eclipsed (which would be 0 degrees) but are instead staggered to a position where they are closer together than in the fully staggered conformation. This results in a dihedral angle of approximately 120 degrees, which is characteristic of the interactions between the groups in this specific conformation.

questions.question: Which of the following conformations will be the most stable?
questions.topicName: Tetravalence of Carbon: Shapes of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: Conformational isomerism is an isomerism in which different structures are formed due to free rotation around carbon — carbon single bond. The structures which are obtained are called conformers of each other and the phenomenon is known as conformational isomerism. Among the given conformations, the most stable conformer would be one in which bulky groups are present opposite to each other, because such conformer has lowest bond angle and torsional strain and it is called as staggerd-conformer.

questions.question: Total number of stereoisomer of compound is
questions.topicName: Isomerism
questions.explanation:
To determine the total number of stereoisomers for the compound, we need to identify the chiral centers and any possible geometric isomerism.
1. Chiral Centers: Count the number of carbon atoms that are bonded to four different substituents. Each chiral center contributes to the number of stereoisomers according to the formula {tex}2^n{/tex}, where {tex}n{/tex} is the number of chiral centers.
2. Geometric Isomerism: If the compound has double bonds or rings that restrict rotation, it may also have cis/trans (or E/Z) isomerism, which adds to the total number of stereoisomers. questions.topicName: Isomerism
questions.explanation:
The solution is correct because:
1. Statement S1: The compound {tex}H_3C-C \equiv CH{/tex} has a triple bond, which restricts rotation around that bond. Therefore, this statement is true.
2. Statement S2: Cyclic compounds typically have restricted rotation due to their ring structure, which prevents free rotation around the bonds. Thus, this statement is also true.
3. Statement S3: The compound {tex}CH_3-CH_3{/tex} (ethane) does not have restricted rotation because it is a single bond, allowing free rotation. Therefore, this statement is false.
4. Statement S4: The compound {tex}H_3C-C \equiv N{/tex} has a triple bond, which restricts rotation. However, geometrical isomerism requires the presence of different groups on either side of the bond, which is not the case here. Thus, this statement is false. The stability order is described with the help of hyperconjugation and inductive effect. On the basis of hyper-conjugation

{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\underset{{\mathrm{CH}}_3}{\overset{\vert+}{\underset\vert{\mathrm C}}}}{/tex} shows nine resonating structures due to the presence of nine {tex}\alpha{/tex}-C-H bonds. Greater the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen atom greater will be the hyperconjugation and thus greater will be the stability.
{tex}{\overset+{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}_3{/tex} does not show any hyperconjugation while {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\overset+{\mathrm C}\mathrm H}_2{/tex} shows three hyperconjugating structures. questions.question: Mechanism involved and product obtained when 3-iodo-3-ethylpentane is treated with sodium methoxide in methanol is :

The mechanism is E2 because it involves a concerted process where the base abstracts a proton while the leaving group departs, leading to the formation of the alkene in a single step. questions.question: The {tex}S_{N} 2{/tex} reactivity order for halides :
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because the reactivity of alkyl halides in nucleophilic substitution reactions (SN2) is influenced by the leaving ability of the halide ions. Iodine (I) is the best leaving group among the halides due to its larger size and weaker bond strength with carbon, making it easier to break away. Bromine (Br) follows, as it is also a good leaving group but not as effective as iodine. Chlorine (Cl) is less effective than bromine, and fluorine (F) is the least effective due to its strong bond with carbon and smaller size, which makes it less stable as a leaving group. Therefore, the order of reactivity for SN2 reactions is indeed R-I > R-Br > R-Cl > R-F.

questions.question: The order of decreasing nucleophilicities of the following species is :
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation:
The solution ranks the nucleophilicity of the species based on their ability to donate electrons.
1. Nucleophilicity and Charge: Negatively charged species (anions) are generally more nucleophilic than neutral species because they have an extra electron to donate. Therefore, the order starts with the anions.
2. Sulfur vs. Oxygen: Among the anions, the thiolate ion (CH{tex}_3{/tex}S-) is more nucleophilic than the alkoxide ion (CH{tex}_3{/tex}O-) because sulfur is larger and more polarizable than oxygen, allowing it to stabilize the negative charge better and donate electrons more readily.
3. Carboxylate Ion: The carboxylate ion (CH{tex}_3{/tex}COO-) is less nucleophilic than both CH{tex}_3{/tex}S- and CH{tex}_3{/tex}O- due to resonance stabilization, which delocalizes the negative charge and makes it less available for nucleophilic attack.
4. Neutral Alcohol: The neutral alcohol (CH{tex}_3{/tex}OH) is the least nucleophilic because it lacks a negative charge and has a more stable structure, making it less reactive in nucleophilic reactions. Thus, the order of decreasing nucleophilicity is correctly identified as CH{tex}_3{/tex}S- > CH{tex}_3{/tex}O- > CH{tex}_3{/tex}COO- > CH{tex}_3{/tex}OH.

questions.question: What will be the order of reactivity of the following carbonyl compounds with Grignard's reagent?
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation:
The order of reactivity of carbonyl compounds with Grignard's reagent is primarily influenced by the steric and electronic factors surrounding the carbonyl group.
1. Steric Hindrance: Compounds with less steric hindrance around the carbonyl carbon are more reactive. This means that less bulky groups attached to the carbonyl will allow Grignard's reagent to approach and react more easily.
2. Electrophilicity: The more electrophilic the carbonyl carbon, the more reactive it is towards nucleophiles like Grignard's reagent. Electron-withdrawing groups increase electrophilicity, while electron-donating groups decrease it. In the given compounds: - Compound I has the least steric hindrance and is likely the most electrophilic, making it the most reactive. - Compound II has moderate steric hindrance and is still quite reactive. - Compound III has more steric hindrance and is less reactive than I and II. - Compound IV has the most steric hindrance and is the least reactive. Thus, the order of reactivity is I > II > III > IV, which aligns with the solution provided.

questions.question:
The bond dissociation energy of the {tex}{C}-{H}{/tex} bond for the compounds

  1. {tex}{CH}_{3}-{H}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2}-{H}{/tex}
  3. {tex}{CH}_{2}={CHCH}_{2}-{H}{/tex}
  4. {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5}-{H}{/tex}

decreases in the order :
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation:
The bond dissociation energy of the C-H bond varies based on the molecular structure and the surrounding atoms. In this case:
1. C6H5-H (IV): The C-H bond in benzene is stabilized by resonance, making it stronger and thus having a higher bond dissociation energy.
2. CH3-H (I): The C-H bond in methane is relatively strong due to the lack of steric hindrance and the presence of only hydrogen atoms.
3. CH3CH2-H (II): The C-H bond here is slightly weaker than in methane due to increased steric effects from the ethyl group, which can lead to some destabilization.
4. CH2=CHCH2-H (III): The C-H bond in this allylic position is the weakest due to the presence of a double bond, which introduces additional electron repulsion and makes the bond easier to break. Thus, the order of bond dissociation energy is influenced by resonance stabilization and steric effects, leading to the conclusion that IV > I > II > III is the correct order.

questions.question: The general order of reactivity of carbonyl compounds for nucleophilic addition reactions is :
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism

The solution is correct because it accurately reflects the general reactivity trend of carbonyl compounds in nucleophilic addition reactions. In this context, the reactivity is influenced by the nature of the substituents attached to the carbonyl carbon.
1. Formaldehyde (H{tex}_2{/tex}C=O) is the most reactive due to the absence of steric hindrance and the presence of only hydrogen atoms, which allows easier access for nucleophiles.
2. Aldehydes (RCHO) are more reactive than ketones because they have one alkyl group, which provides some steric hindrance but is still less than that of ketones.
3. Aromatic aldehydes (ArCHO) are less reactive than aliphatic aldehydes due to the electron-withdrawing nature of the aromatic ring, which stabilizes the carbonyl and makes it less susceptible to nucleophilic attack.
4. Ketones (R{tex}_2{/tex}C=O) are less reactive than aldehydes because they have two alkyl groups, which create more steric hindrance and electron-donating effects that stabilize the carbonyl.
5. Aromatic ketones (Ar{tex}_2{/tex}C=O) are the least reactive due to the combined effects of steric hindrance from the two aromatic groups and the electron-withdrawing nature of the aromatic rings. This order of reactivity aligns with the provided solution, confirming its correctness.

The solution is correct because it considers the reactivity of the carbonyl compounds towards nucleophilic addition by HCN.
1. PhCOPh (d): This compound has a carbonyl group that is stabilized by resonance with two phenyl groups, making it less reactive towards nucleophiles like HCN.
2. PhCOCH3 (c): This compound has one phenyl group, which still provides some resonance stabilization, but is more reactive than PhCOPh due to the presence of the alkyl group, which is less electron-withdrawing than a phenyl group.
3. CH3COCH3 (b): This ketone has no resonance stabilization from aromatic groups, making it more reactive than the previous two compounds. The two methyl groups provide some electron-donating effect, increasing the reactivity.
4. HCHO (a): Formaldehyde is the most reactive due to the lack of steric hindrance and resonance stabilization, allowing for easier attack by the nucleophile. Thus, the order of reactivity from least to most reactive is correctly identified as d < c < b < a.

questions.question: The elimination of HX from an alkyl halide forms an alkene. The order of the elimination reaction is :

Thus, the order of reactivity in elimination reactions is indeed 3° > 2° > 1°.

questions.question: In the elimination of reactions, that is, in the formation of alkenes, the reactivity of halogens in alkyl halides is in the order :

questions.question: The products obtained via oxymercuration demercuration of 1-butyne would be :

The oxymercuration-demercuration reaction involves the addition of water across a triple bond in an alkyne, resulting in the formation of a carbonyl compound. In the case of 1-butyne, the reaction proceeds as follows:
1. The triple bond of 1-butyne reacts with mercuric acetate in the presence of water, leading to the formation of an intermediate mercurial alcohol.
2. This intermediate then undergoes demercuration, typically using sodium borohydride, to yield the final product. For 1-butyne, the oxymercuration step adds a hydroxyl group and a hydrogen atom across the triple bond, resulting in a carbonyl compound. The product formed is butan-2-one (or methyl ethyl ketone), which corresponds to the structure given in option  as CH2CH2COCH3. Thus, the correct product from the oxymercuration-demercuration of 1-butyne is indeed CH2CH2COCH3.

questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism

In this reaction, the base abstracts a proton {tex}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right){/tex}from the {tex}\beta{/tex}-carbon - the carbon adjacent to the one bearing the halogen atom. Simultaneously, the halide ion leaves from the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-carbon (the carbon directly attached to the halogen), resulting in the formation of an alkene.

When ozonolysis occurs, it breaks this double bond, leading to the formation of two carbonyl compounds: acetone (from the central carbon) and propionaldehyde (from the terminal carbon). The fact that these products are in equimolar amounts indicates that the starting material must have a symmetrical structure, which is characteristic of 2-Methyl-2-pentene. questions.question: which is most easily dehydrohalogenated :

questions.explanation: The compounds shown are different isomers of chlorotoluene: I (ortho), II (meta), and III (para). In dehydrohalogenation, the rate depends on how easily the {tex}\beta{/tex}-hydrogen can be removed and how stable the transition state and resulting product are. The methyl group is an electron-donating group, and in the ortho-position (I), it can stabilize the transition state more effectively through hyperconjugation and inductive effects, facilitating easier elimination of HCl . In meta (II), this stabilization is weaker, and in para (III), the effect is more delocalized and less significant. Therefore, compound I is most easily dehydrohalogenated due to favorable electronic and steric conditions.

questions.question: Arrange the following alkanols {tex}{a}, {b}{/tex} and c in order of their reactivity towards acid catalysed dehydration :
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation:
In acid-catalyzed dehydration of alcohols, the reactivity depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed after the loss of water. Tertiary alcohols form the most stable carbocations, followed by secondary, and then primary. Among the given alcohols:
(b) is a tertiary alcohol, so it undergoes dehydration most easily.
(c) is a secondary alcohol, so it is next in reactivity.
(a) is a primary alcohol, which forms the least stable carbocation and is least reactive toward dehydration.
Therefore, the correct order of reactivity is: (b > c > a).

questions.question: A hydrocarbon on reaction with concentrated {tex}{KMnO}_{4}{/tex} solution produces propionic acid only, but on reaction with cold dilute {tex}{KMnO}_{4}{/tex} produces a diketone. The hydrocarbon is :
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation: The solution identifies 3-hexyne as the correct hydrocarbon based on its reactions with concentrated and dilute KMnO4. When 3-hexyne reacts with concentrated KMnO4, it undergoes oxidative cleavage, resulting in the formation of propionic acid. This indicates that the triple bond is being cleaved to produce carboxylic acids. In contrast, when 3-hexyne reacts with cold dilute KMnO4, it undergoes a mild oxidation that leads to the formation of a diketone. This suggests that the triple bond is being oxidized without complete cleavage, resulting in a compound with two carbonyl groups. The other options do not fit these reaction outcomes. 3-hexene would not produce propionic acid under these conditions, and 1-butyne and 1-butene would not yield the same products as 3-hexyne. Thus, the behavior of 3-hexyne aligns perfectly with the described reactions.

questions.question: Alcohols undergo dehydration in the following sequence :

This is because dehydration (usually under acidic conditions and heat) proceeds via the E1 mechanism, which involves the formation of a carbocation intermediate. Tertiary carbocations are the most stable due to inductive and hyperconjugative effects, making tertiary alcohols dehydrate more readily. Secondary carbocations are moderately stable, and primary carbocations are the least stable, making primary alcohols hardest to dehydrate.

The second step with {tex}\mathrm{NaNH}_2{/tex} (a strong base) ensures complete removal of both bromine atoms by further dehydrohalogenation, confirming the formation of the alkyne.

Hence, hydrolysis of 2,2-dichloropropane yields acetaldehyde, not diol or alcohol.

questions.question: The reactivities of {tex}{CH}_{3} {Cl}, {CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {CH}_{2} {Cl}{/tex} and chlorobenzene towards {tex}{S}_{{N}^{2}}{/tex} reaction are in the order :
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation: In an SN2 reaction, reactivity depends on steric hindrance around the carbon bearing the leaving group: the less hindered, the more reactive. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Cl}{/tex} (methyl chloride) has no alkyl groups attached to the carbon, so it is the least hindered and thus most reactive. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}{/tex} (1-chloropropane) is primary and slightly more hindered but still reactive. Chlorobenzene {tex}\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right){/tex} is almost unreactive in SN 2 reactions because the carbonchlorine bond has partial double-bond character due to resonance, making it very difficult to displace the chlorine. Therefore, the reactivity order is: {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Cl}{/tex}.

questions.question:
The order of decreasing {tex}S_{N} 1{/tex} reactivities of the halides is :

  1. {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {CH}_{2} {Cl}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{CH}_{2}={CHCHClCH}_{3}{/tex}
  3. {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {CHClCH}_{3}{/tex}
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation: The SN1 reaction proceeds via a carbocation intermediate, so the stability of the carbocation formed after the leaving group departs is crucial. In compound III {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{C}^{+} \mathrm{HCH}_3\right){/tex}, a secondary carbocation is formed, which is stabilized by hyperconjugation and inductive effects, making it the most reactive. Compound II {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CHC}^{+} \mathrm{HCH}_3\right){/tex} forms an allylic carbocation, which is also stabilized by resonance but slightly less stable than the secondary carbocation in III. Compound I ( {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CI}{/tex} ) forms a primary carbocation, which is highly unstable and thus the least reactive. Therefore, the correct order of SN1 reactivity is: III > II > I.

questions.question: Which of the following is most stable resonance structure?

By analyzing the provided structures based on these criteria, the most stable resonance structure will be the one that adheres best to the octet rule, has minimal formal charges, and has charges placed appropriately according to electronegativity. questions.question: Which one of the following has inductive, mesomeric and hyperconjugation effect?
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation:
The correct option is CH3CH=O || CH–C–CH3 because it exhibits all three effects: inductive, mesomeric, and hyperconjugation.
1. Inductive Effect: The presence of the carbonyl group (C=O) in CH3CH=O induces a slight positive charge on the adjacent carbon, affecting the electron density in the molecule.
2. Mesomeric Effect: The carbonyl group can participate in resonance with the adjacent double bond, allowing for delocalization of electrons. This resonance stabilizes the molecule and is characteristic of mesomeric effects.
3. Hyperconjugation: The presence of alkyl groups (like CH3) adjacent to the double bond allows for hyperconjugation, where the σ-bonds can interact with the {tex}\pi{/tex}-bond, further stabilizing the molecule. The other options do not exhibit all three effects simultaneously, making the only correct choice.

questions.question: Which one of the following is most basic?
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation:
To determine which compound is the most basic, we need to consider the structure and the presence of electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups in each molecule. Basicity is influenced by the ability of a compound to donate a pair of electrons to accept protons (H+).
1. The first compound has a nitrogen atom that is part of a cyclic structure, which may limit its ability to donate electrons due to steric hindrance or resonance effects.
2. The second compound has a nitrogen atom with an alkyl group, which is electron-donating and enhances basicity.
3. The third compound has a nitrogen atom that is part of an aromatic system, which typically decreases basicity due to resonance stabilization of the lone pair.
4. The fourth compound has a nitrogen atom with no significant electron-withdrawing groups, making it more basic. Comparing these factors, the second compound is likely the most basic due to the presence of an alkyl group that increases electron density on the nitrogen, enhancing its ability to accept protons.

questions.question: What is the decreasing order of strength of the bases , {tex}{OH}^{-}{/tex}(I), {tex}{NH}_{2}^{-}{/tex}(II), {tex}{H}-{C} \equiv {C}^{-}{/tex}(III), {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2}{ }^{-}{/tex}(IV)?

2. Amide ion (II) is also a strong base, but less so than alkyl anions because it has a nitrogen atom that can stabilize the negative charge through resonance.
3. Alkynyl anion (III) is a weaker base than the amide ion because the negative charge is held by a sp-hybridized carbon, which is more electronegative and less willing to donate electrons.
4. Hydroxide ion (I) is the weakest base among these options, as it is a stable ion and less reactive compared to the others. Thus, the order of strength is IV > II > III > I, confirming the solution provided.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements

  1. {tex}{CH}_{3} {O} \stackrel{\oplus}{{C}} {H}_{2}{/tex} is more stable than {tex}{CH}_{3} \stackrel{\oplus}{{C}} {H}_{2}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{Me}_{2} \stackrel{\oplus}{{C}}{/tex} is more stable than {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} \stackrel{\oplus}{{C}} {H}_{2}{/tex}
  3. {tex}{CH}_{2}={CH}-\stackrel{\oplus}{{C}} {H}_{2}{/tex} is more stable than {tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} \stackrel{\oplus}{{C}} {H}_{2}{/tex}
  4. {tex}{CH}_{2}=\stackrel{\oplus}{{C}} {H}{/tex} is more stable than {tex}{CH}_{3} \stackrel{\oplus}{{C}} {H}{/tex} of these statements

options.option: b, c and d are correct

questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation: Statements (a), (b), and (c) are correct. In (a), {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OCH}_2{ }^{+}{/tex}is more stable than {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2{ }^{+}{/tex} because oxygen donates electron density by +M (resonance) effect, stabilizing the carbocation. In (b), {tex}\mathrm{Me}_2 \mathrm{C}^{+}{/tex}is a tertiary carbocation, which is more stable than the primary {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2{ }^{+}{/tex}due to strong hyperconjugation and inductive effects. In (c), the allyl carbocation {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_2{ }^{+}\right){/tex}is resonance-stabilized over three carbons, making it more stable than a simple primary carbocation. However, (d) is incorrect because the propargyl carbocation {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_2 \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_2{ }^{+}\right){/tex}is less stable than allylic or tertiary carbocations; the adjacent sp-hybridized carbon withdraws electron density, destabilizing the positive charge. Therefore, the correct answer is a, b, and c are correct.

Next, {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OCHCH}_3{ }^{+}{/tex}also benefits from oxygen's resonance but slightly less than {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OCH}_2{ }^{+}{/tex}due to substitution. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}^{+} \mathrm{CH}_3{/tex} is a secondary carbocation, stabilized by hyperconjugation and inductive effects, while {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2{ }^{+}{/tex}is only a primary carbocation, which is the least stable among them due to lack of significant stabilization. questions.explanation: 2-methyl-2-butene has a specific structure that allows for the identification of alkenyl groups. An alkenyl group is a substituent derived from an alkene by removing one hydrogen atom. In the case of 2-methyl-2-butene, the molecule has three distinct positions where alkenyl groups can be formed due to the presence of double bonds in the structure. Therefore, the correct answer is that it gives 3 alkenyl groups.

questions.question: 1 - butyne gives ________ alkynyl groups
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation:
1-butyne is a terminal alkyne with the formula C4H6. It can form three distinct alkynyl groups based on the position of the triple bond. The alkynyl groups are derived from the removal of a hydrogen atom from the terminal carbon, leading to three possible structures:
1. The alkynyl group from the first carbon (C≡C-CH2-CH3).
2. The alkynyl group from the second carbon (C≡C-CH3-CH2).
3. The alkynyl group from the third carbon (C≡C-CH2-CH2).
Thus, there are three unique alkynyl groups that can be formed from 1-butyne.

questions.question: 2 - pentyne gives ________ alkynyl groups
questions.topicName: Fundamental Concepts In Organic Reaction Mechanism
questions.explanation: 2-pentyne is a symmetrical alkyne with the formula C5H8. It has a triple bond between the second and third carbon atoms. The alkynyl groups are formed by removing one hydrogen atom from the terminal carbon atoms of the alkyne. In 2-pentyne, there are two terminal carbon atoms (the first and the fifth carbon), and each can form an alkynyl group. Additionally, the triple bond allows for the formation of a third unique alkynyl group by considering the position of the triple bond itself. Thus, there are three distinct alkynyl groups that can be derived from 2-pentyne, which makes the answer 3.

questions.question: The possible number of alkenyl radicals obtained form butene :

questions.explanation: The solution is correct because butene has four carbon atoms and can exist in different structural forms (isomers). Each isomer can generate alkenyl radicals by removing a hydrogen atom from the double bond or from adjacent carbon atoms. For butene, the possible structural isomers are 1-butene, 2-butene (which has two geometric isomers: cis and trans), and isobutene. Each of these can form different alkenyl radicals, leading to a total of eight distinct radicals when considering all possible positions for the unpaired electron. Thus, the total number of alkenyl radicals formed from butene is indeed 8.

questions.question: The principle involved in gas-liquid chromatography (GLC) is:
questions.topicName: Methods of Purification of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution is incorrect. The principle involved in gas-liquid chromatography (GLC) is actually partition. In GLC, the separation of components occurs based on their distribution between a stationary liquid phase and a mobile gas phase. This means that the components partition between the two phases, leading to their separation as they travel through the column.

questions.question: Chloroform and aniline can be purified by:
questions.topicName: Methods of Purification of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because chloroform and aniline can be separated based on their different boiling points through distillation. Distillation is an effective method for purifying liquids, especially when they have significantly different boiling points, allowing for the separation of the components based on their volatility.

questions.question: Principle of the crystallization method depends on:
questions.topicName: Methods of Purification of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The crystallization method primarily relies on the solubility difference of substances. This technique involves dissolving a mixture in a solvent at a high temperature and then allowing it to cool. As the temperature decreases, the solubility of the solute changes, leading to the formation of crystals. This process effectively separates the solute from impurities based on their differing solubilities in the solvent. Other options, such as boiling point or vapor pressure differences, are not the main principles behind crystallization.

questions.question: The mixture of urea and naphthalene can be separated by:
questions.topicName: Methods of Purification of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because naphthalene can sublimate, meaning it can transition from a solid to a gas without becoming a liquid, while urea does not sublimate under normal conditions. By heating the mixture, naphthalene will vaporize, allowing it to be separated from the non-sublimable urea. This method effectively separates the two substances based on their physical properties.

questions.question: CFCs are also known as:
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: CFCs, or chlorofluorocarbons, are commonly referred to as freons. This term specifically describes a group of chemicals used primarily as refrigerants and in aerosol propellants. The other options listed do not accurately describe CFCs, making "freons" the correct answer.

questions.question: Which of the following reaction(s) is/are carried out during the formation of {tex}{O}_{3}{/tex} layer in stratosphere?
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The formation of the ozone layer in the stratosphere involves several key reactions. Reaction describes the photodissociation of molecular oxygen (O{tex}_2{/tex}) by ultraviolet (UV) light, producing individual oxygen atoms (O). This is a crucial step as it generates the reactive species needed for ozone formation.Another Reaction shows how these oxygen atoms react with molecular oxygen (O{tex}_2{/tex}) in the presence of UV light to form ozone (O{tex}_3{/tex}). This is the primary reaction for ozone formation. Other Reaction illustrates the photodissociation of ozone (O{tex}_3{/tex}) back into molecular oxygen (O{tex}_2{/tex}) and oxygen atoms (O). This reaction is part of the dynamic equilibrium in the ozone layer, where ozone is both formed and broken down. Since all three reactions are involved in the processes that create and maintain the ozone layer, the correct answer is that all of the reactions are carried out during the formation of the ozone layer.

questions.question: Classical smog is a mixture of:
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because classical smog is primarily formed from the combination of smoke and fog, along with sulfur dioxide (SO2) produced from burning fossil fuels. This mixture creates a dense, polluted air condition typical of industrial areas. The presence of fog enhances the visibility of smoke particles, leading to the characteristic appearance of smog.

questions.question: What health hazard is caused by the used of leaded petrol?

options.option: Breathing problems and other lungs diseases
Abnormal development of red blood cell

questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because lead exposure from leaded petrol primarily affects the blood, leading to conditions such as anemia due to the abnormal development of red blood cells. While lead can also contribute to other health issues, the most direct and significant impact related to leaded petrol is on blood cell development.

questions.question: Nitrogen detection in an organic compound is carried out by Lassaigne's test. The blue colour formed corresponds to which of the following formulae?
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The blue color observed in Lassaigne's test for nitrogen detection is due to the formation of a complex between iron and cyanide ions. The correct formula corresponds to the complex that forms when iron reacts with the cyanide produced from the organic compound containing nitrogen. In this case, the formula {tex}\text{Fe}_4[\text{Fe}(\text{CN})_6]_3{/tex} indicates that there are four iron ions coordinating with three hexacyanoferrate ions, which is consistent with the stoichiometry required to produce the characteristic blue color. This complex is known as Turnbull's blue, which is specifically formed under these conditions.

questions.question: Which is a not correct match?
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution identifies option as incorrect because Lassaigne's test is used to detect the presence of nitrogen and halogens in organic compounds, not oxygen. The other methods listed correctly correspond to their respective elements or compounds: Carius method for halogens, Liebig's method for carbon and hydrogen, and Kjeldahl's method for nitrogen.

questions.question: F cannot be tested by "Lassaigne's test" because
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because Lassaigne's test involves the formation of a precipitate when a sample is heated with sodium metal and then treated with water. In the case of fluorides, particularly AgF, it is soluble in water, which means it will not form a precipitate. This solubility prevents the detection of fluorine through the typical method used in Lassaigne's test, making option AgF is soluble in H2O the correct reason why F cannot be tested by this method.

questions.question: In Duma's method for determining the nitrogen content of an organic compound, the nitrogen content is determined in the form of:
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because Duma's method involves the combustion of an organic compound in the presence of copper oxide, which converts nitrogen in the sample into nitrogen gas (N2). This gas is then collected and measured, allowing for the determination of the nitrogen content in the original compound. Other options do not represent the form in which nitrogen is measured in this method.

questions.question: The function of boiling the sodium extract with concentrated nitric acid before testing halogens is:
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The solution is correct because boiling the sodium extract with concentrated nitric acid serves to eliminate interfering ions such as cyanide (CN⁻) and sulfide (S²⁻). These ions can react with halogens and produce false positives or obscure the results during halogen testing. By destroying these ions, the test can accurately identify the presence of halogens without interference.

questions.question:
The violet-red colour in the Lassaigne's test of sulphur is due to
{tex}{S}^{2-}+\left[\begin{array}{c}{Fe}({CN})_{5}({NO})^{2-} \\ \binom{\text { Sodiumnitro }}{\text { prusside }}\end{array} \rightarrow\right.{/tex}
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The violet-red color observed in Lassaigne's test for sulfur is specifically due to the formation of a complex between sulfide ions and a particular iron complex. In this case, the correct answer is {tex}\text{Na}_4\left[\text{Fe}(\text{CN})_5 \text{NOS}\right]{/tex}, which indicates that the iron complex is reacting with the sulfide ions to produce the characteristic color. The other options do not represent the correct complex that forms in the presence of sulfide ions, which is why they are not correct. The formation of the specific iron-sulfur complex is what leads to the violet-red coloration.

questions.question: Which of the following compound will give blood-red colour while doing the Lassaigne's test for N?
questions.topicName: Qualitative Analysis of Organic Compounds
questions.explanation: The Lassaigne's test is used to detect the presence of nitrogen in organic compounds. When a compound containing nitrogen is heated with sodium metal, it forms sodium cyanide, which can then react with iron(III) ions to produce a colored complex. In this case, the compound (2) {tex}H_2N(C_6H_4)SO_3H{/tex} contains an amino group (-NH2) that can release nitrogen upon heating. This nitrogen can form sodium cyanide, which will subsequently react with iron(III) ions to produce a blood-red color. The other compounds either do not contain nitrogen or do not have the necessary functional groups to produce the blood-red color in the Lassaigne's test.

questions.question: Which type of smog occur in warm, dry and sunny climate?
questions.topicName: Quantitative Analysis
questions.explanation: Photochemical smog occurs in warm, dry, and sunny climates because it is formed when sunlight reacts with pollutants like nitrogen oxides and volatile organic compounds. This reaction creates secondary pollutants, including ozone, which are more prevalent in such conditions. Therefore, the correct answer is photochemical smog.

questions.question: 0.5 g of an organic compound containing N on kjeldahlising required 29 ml of {tex}{N} / 5\ {H}_{2} {SO}_{4}{/tex} for complete neutralization of NH3. The percentage of N in the compound is:
questions.topicName: Quantitative Analysis
questions.explanation: To find the percentage of nitrogen (N) in the organic compound, we first need to determine the amount of ammonia (NH{tex}_3{/tex}) produced during the Kjeldahl process. The volume of N/5 H{tex}_2{/tex}SO{tex}_4{/tex} used for neutralization is given as 29 ml. N/5 H{tex}_2{/tex}SO{tex}_4{/tex} means that the concentration of sulfuric acid is 1/5 of normal strength, which is equivalent to 0.2 N. The number of equivalents of H{tex}_2{/tex}SO{tex}_4{/tex} used can be calculated as: {tex}{Equivalent \ of \ H_2SO_4} = \text{Volume (L)} \times \text{Normality} {/tex} {tex}= 0.029 \, \text{L} \times 0.2 \, \text{N} = 0.0058 \, \text{equivalents}{/tex} Since each equivalent of H{tex}_2{/tex}SO{tex}_4{/tex} neutralizes one equivalent of NH{tex}_3{/tex}, we have 0.0058 equivalents of NH{tex}_3{/tex}. The molecular weight of nitrogen (N) is approximately 14 g/mol, so the mass of nitrogen can be calculated as: {tex}\text{Mass of N} = \text{Equivalents of N} \times \text{Molar mass of N} = 0.0058 \times 14 \, \text{g} = 0.0812 \, \text{g}{/tex} Next, we calculate the percentage of nitrogen in the organic compound: {tex}\text{Percentage of N} = \left( \frac{\text{Mass of N}}{\text{Mass of compound}} \right) \times 100 = \left( \frac{0.0812 \, \text{g}}{0.5 \, \text{g}} \right) \times 100 = 16.24\%{/tex} Rounding this value gives approximately 16.2%. Thus, the percentage of nitrogen in the compound is correctly calculated as 16.2%.

questions.question: If 0.2 g of an organic compound on complete combustion produces 0.18 g of water then % of hydrogen in it is:
questions.topicName: Quantitative Analysis
questions.explanation:
To find the percentage of hydrogen in the organic compound, we first need to determine the amount of hydrogen in the water produced. Water (H2O) has a molar mass of approximately 18 g/mol, and each molecule of water contains 2 hydrogen atoms. From the problem, 0.18 g of water is produced. The mass of hydrogen in this water can be calculated as follows:
1. Calculate the number of moles of water: {tex}\text{Moles of water} = \frac{0.18 \text{ g}}{18 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.01 \text{ moles}{/tex}
2. Since each mole of water contains 2 moles of hydrogen, the moles of hydrogen is: {tex}\text{Moles of hydrogen} = 2 \times 0.01 = 0.02 \text{ moles}{/tex}
3. Now, calculate the mass of hydrogen: {tex}\text{Mass of hydrogen} = 0.02 \text{ moles} \times 1 \text{ g/mol} = 0.02 \text{ g}{/tex}
4. To find the percentage of hydrogen in the original compound, use the formula: {tex}\% \text{ of hydrogen} = \left( \frac{\text{mass of hydrogen}}{\text{mass of compound}} \right) \times 100{/tex} {tex}\% \text{ of hydrogen} = \left( \frac{0.02 \text{ g}}{0.2 \text{ g}} \right) \times 100 = 10\%{/tex}
Thus, the percentage of hydrogen in the organic compound is 10%.

questions.question: Cobalt chloride when dissolved in water forms pink colored complex {tex}\underline{X}{/tex} which has octahedral geometry. This solution on treating with conc. HCl forms deep blue complex, {tex}\underline{{Y}}{/tex} which has a {tex}\underline{{Z}}{/tex} geometry. {tex}X, Y{/tex} and {tex}Z{/tex}, respectively, are
questions.topicName: Quantitative Analysis
questions.explanation:
{tex}{X}=\left[{Co}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+},({Y})=\left[{CoCl}_{4}\right]^{2-},({Z})={/tex} Tetrahedral
The {tex}\left[{Co}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}{/tex} when treated with excess HCl (conc.) turns deep blue in color. This is because the complex absorbs an orange light (low-frequency light). This is due to the lower splitting of d-orbitals of {tex}{Co}^{2+}{/tex} ions. The tetrahedral complexes have a lower splitting than the octahedral complexes and absorb light of lower frequency. Hence, the geometry of the complex {tex}({Y}){/tex} is tetrahedral.

• The solvent extraction method is used to separate liquids with the difference in densities. The density of {tex}{CH}_{2} {Cl}_{2}{/tex} is greater than the density of water.
  • The p-nitrophenol has intermolecular H-bonding. Hence, it is separated by column chromatography.
  • Kerosene and naphthalene are separated by fractional distillation due to the difference in the boiling points.
  • NaCl is an ionic compound and {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{12} {O}_{6}{/tex} is a covalent compound. {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{12} {O}_{6}{/tex} is soluble in an organic solvent. Hence, they can be separated by crystallization.
